names.scientific_name: Tocopherols (mixture of alpha, beta, gamma, delta forms)

natural_occurrence.description: Vitamin E exists in eight forms: four tocopherols (alpha, beta, gamma, delta) and four tocotrienols. E306 is a mixture rich in tocopherols extracted from natural sources, primarily vegetable oils.

manufacturing.description: Commonly extracted from the distillate obtained during deodorization of vegetable oils (especially soybean, canola, sunflower, corn, cottonseed). Soybean oil deodorized distillate is the primary source due to low cost. Can also be extracted using supercritical CO2 from wheat germ.

eu.conditions: Evaluated together with E307, E308, E309

usa.notes: Approved as antioxidant and nutrient supplement

regulatory_history.event: EFSA concludes no safety concern at reported uses and levels; notes vitamin E is essential nutrient

adi_context.source_note: EFSA concluded available data too limited to establish ADI, but no safety concern at reported uses and levels. Vitamin E is an essential nutrient widely consumed via food.

fun_facts.title: Essential Nutrient Disguised as Additive
fun_facts.description: Unlike most food additives, tocopherols (Vitamin E) are actually essential nutrients that humans must obtain from diet - they're beneficial compounds, not just preservatives!

fun_facts.title: Nature's Own Antioxidant
fun_facts.description: Found naturally in the same oils it's used to protect. When you eat nuts, seeds, or vegetable oils, you're consuming both the fats AND nature's built-in protection system.

fun_facts.title: Multiple Forms Working Together
fun_facts.description: Exists in 8 different forms (4 tocopherols, 4 tocotrienols), each with slightly different antioxidant powers. Mixed tocopherols provide broader protection than single forms.

fun_facts.title: Extracted from Waste
fun_facts.description: Economically extracted from deodorizer distillate - a by-product of vegetable oil refining - turning what was once waste into valuable vitamin E.

fun_facts.title: Diet Geography
fun_facts.description: α-tocopherol dominates European diets (olive/sunflower oils), while γ-tocopherol is most common in American diets (soybean/corn oils) - your vitamin E profile depends on your cooking oil culture!
